skill.name: competitor-teardown
skill.description: Guides structured competitor research using feature matrices, pricing comparisons, SWOT analysis, positioning maps, review mining, and UX screenshots.
skill.summary: Build competitive teardowns with research prompts, comparison frameworks, and report templates.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The skill is a Markdown guide, but it instructs users to pipe a remote installer into a shell and to run multiple external inference.sh commands. I found no evidence of confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection, but the install and remote execution patterns are unsafe enough to require publisher changes before marketplace release.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start tells users to run a remote install script through sh. This is a true positive for the static pipe-to-shell finding because compromised DNS, TLS, hosting, or script contents could execute arbitrary code on the user's machine.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command directly pipes content from https://cli.inference.sh into sh. The surrounding install note does not remove the execution risk at install time.

high_findings.title: Remote Code Execution Service Recommended
high_findings.description: The skill recommends sending Python code to infsh/python-executor. The example code is benign, but the pattern delegates code execution to an external service and could expose user data or generated artifacts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The use of a hosted Python executor is explicit and semantically confirmed. The sample is not malicious, so the finding is high risk rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Surface
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash commands matching infsh * and provides many examples that call external apps. This confirms the external command risk factor, although commands are limited to the inference.sh CLI namespace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command blocks are direct evidence. The risk is moderated because the shell permission is scoped to infsh commands.

medium_findings.title: External Web Research And Screenshot Collection
medium_findings.description: The guide instructs users to query search services, extract web pages, and capture screenshots through external inference.sh apps. This is expected for competitor research, but users may send private competitor lists, strategy terms, or URLs to third-party services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly send queries and URLs to external apps. The behavior is functional for the skill, but privacy impact depends on user input.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Functional Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh installation resources and placeholder competitor pages. These are true network indicators but not evidence of malicious exfiltration by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and align with the skill's stated purpose. I found no evidence that they carry secrets or hidden payloads.

low_findings.title: Static False Positives In Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance findings appear to be false positives from ordinary text and sample tables. For example, SSO/SAML is a feature name, not Windows SAM database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are Markdown examples or normal competitive analysis guidance. I found no credential file access, cryptographic implementation, or local system enumeration logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Piped To sh
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads a script and immediately executes it with sh. Replace this with a manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution steps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is the exact pipe-to-shell pattern. It is dangerous even when the stated source is trusted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted Executor Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill routes Python code through infsh/python-executor. Marketplace reviewers should require a disclosure that code and inputs are sent to an external execution service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The executor name and code payload are explicit. The example is benign but the execution model is still sensitive.

content.value_statement: Competitive research often becomes scattered across notes, screenshots, and pricing pages. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable teardown framework for market, product, pricing, and positioning analysis.
actual_capabilities: Defines a seven-layer competitor analysis framework covering product, pricing, positioning, traction, reviews, content, and team.
Provides command examples for web search, page extraction, screenshots, review mining, and image stitching through inference.sh apps.
Includes templates for feature matrices, pricing comparisons, SWOT analysis, positioning maps, and executive summaries.
Guides review mining across G2, Capterra, app stores, Product Hunt, Reddit, and similar sources.
Shows how to create visual deliverables such as positioning maps and screenshot comparison grids.
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI access for the command-based research and screenshot workflows.
Does not verify the accuracy of third-party market data or search results.
May send research queries, URLs, screenshots, or code to external services.
Needs human judgment to avoid biased conclusions and outdated competitor information.

use_cases.title: Prepare Product Strategy Research
use_cases.description: Compare direct competitors across features, pricing, reviews, and positioning before roadmap planning.

use_cases.title: Build Sales Enablement Briefs
use_cases.description: Create concise competitor summaries that explain where your product wins and where competitors are stronger.

use_cases.title: Support Investor Or Board Updates
use_cases.description: Turn market research into executive summaries, positioning maps, and evidence-backed opportunity statements.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Basic Competitor List
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the competitor-teardown framework to identify 3 direct competitors for [product]. Summarize their category, target customer, main offer, and likely positioning.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Features And Pricing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a feature and pricing matrix for [our product] against [competitor A], [competitor B], and [competitor C]. Separate verified facts from assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Mine Reviews For Opportunities
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze public reviews for [competitor]. Extract repeated praise, repeated complaints, switching triggers, and opportunities for our product messaging.

prompt_templates.title: Produce An Executive Teardown
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a full competitive teardown for [market]. Include the seven-layer analysis, SWOT for each competitor, positioning map rationale, pricing implications, and strategic recommendations.

output_examples.input: Compare three project management competitors for a mid-market SaaS product.
output_examples.output: A concise landscape summary with a feature matrix, pricing notes, review themes, positioning rationale, and three recommended differentiation opportunities.

output_examples.input: Analyze customer complaints about a competitor before a product launch.
output: Top complaint themes grouped by frequency and evidence source.
Messaging opportunities that connect those complaints to your product strengths.
Risks where the competitor still has a clear advantage.

output_examples.output: A one-page executive brief covering market size, key competitors, pricing movements, traction signals, and the most important strategic implication.

best_practices: Date every research source and refresh the analysis before major decisions.
Separate verified facts, reasoned estimates, and open questions.
Include competitor strengths so the final teardown remains credible.
anti_patterns: Do not treat search snippets as confirmed market data.
Do not include confidential strategy terms in external search or screenshot tools.
Do not compare too many competitors in one report.

faq.question: What kind of skill is this?
faq.answer: It is a research workflow for competitive analysis, product positioning, pricing comparison, and market landscape summaries.

faq.question: Does it require external tools?
faq.answer: Yes. The command examples rely on inference.sh apps for web search, extraction, screenshots, image stitching, and Python execution.

faq.question: Can it create visual deliverables?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for positioning maps and screenshot comparison grids.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow can send queries, URLs, screenshots, and code to external services.

faq.question: What outputs should I expect?
faq.answer: Typical outputs include feature matrices, SWOT tables, pricing comparisons, review themes, positioning maps, and executive summaries.

faq.question: How many competitors should I analyze?
faq.answer: The skill recommends focusing on three to five direct competitors for a useful and readable report.
